profile.summary: A Product Design Lead sets design direction for a product area while still shaping key work hands-on. They align designers, product managers, engineers, and researchers around user needs, quality, and delivery tradeoffs. They often coach other designers, run critiques, influence roadmap decisions, and represent design in cross-functional planning, while being accountable for experience quality across multiple initiatives.

known_challenges.text: Balancing hands-on design work with coaching, reviews, and cross-functional leadership.
Protecting user experience quality when roadmap pressure pushes speed over craft.
Giving clear direction without becoming a bottleneck for every design decision.
Carrying invisible coordination work across product, engineering, and research.
Handling ambiguity when strategy, scope, or priorities shift mid-stream.
Supporting designers through feedback and growth while managing their own workload.

recommended_question_themes.why: This role constantly trades off craft, speed, and scope, and those tensions shape both outcomes and stress.
Lead-level designers often absorb feedback, critique, and unblock work for others, which affects sustainability and team growth.
They need enough clarity on who decides what to lead effectively across product and engineering.

recommended_question_themes.theme: Growing from strong designer to design leader
recommended_question_themes.why: Success at this level depends on influence, delegation, and judgment, not just personal output.
The quality of relationships with product and engineering strongly affects design impact and execution.
If all time goes to delivery and reviews, this role can lose the forward-looking work expected at lead level.

terminology.meaning: Shared components and patterns for consistent product design.
The steps a user takes to complete a task.
How screens behave and respond to user actions.
Designing so more people can use the product well.
Ongoing learning from users during product development.
Using AI tools to speed concepting or mockups.
Structured feedback on work to improve quality and reasoning.
The guiding approach for experience decisions across work.
Handing off ownership clearly without losing alignment.
Working through differing priorities across partners and leaders.
The standard work should meet before shipping.
Influencing what gets built and when.
Close partnership between design, product, and engineering leads.
A conscious choice between competing goals or constraints.
The key work that determines progress or launch timing.
Work spanning problem finding through shipped execution.
Experience issues left unresolved due to time or scope.
A guiding long-term product or experience outcome.

listen_for: Whether they talk mostly about their own screens or about enabling other designers.
How they describe tradeoffs with product and engineering under delivery pressure.
Whether critique and feedback sound energising, draining, or conflict-heavy.
Shifts from craft language to prioritisation, influence, and decision-rights language.
Whether they can name where design is shaping strategy versus only reacting to tickets.
avoid: Treating them like a pure people manager if they still carry major hands-on design responsibility.
Assuming design delays are only execution problems rather than priority or alignment problems.
Pushing them to attend every review and decision, which turns them into a bottleneck.
Reducing their impact to visual polish instead of product judgment, user insight, and cross-functional influence.
